mnemonics.title: KICOP - Token Types
mnemonics.content: KICOP stands for Keywords, Identifiers, Constants, Operators, Punctuation - the five fundamental token types in C that form the building blocks of every program.

mnemonics.title: IFDCV - Basic Data Types
mnemonics.content: IFDCV stands for Int, Float, Double, Char, Void - the five primary data types in C that determine the type and size of data a variable can hold.

mnemonics.title: RULES for Variable Names
mnemonics.content: RULES stands for: R - Start with letter/underscore, U - Use only alphanumeric and underscore, L - Case-sensitive, E - Cannot be keyword, S - No spaces/special chars allowed.

mnemonics.title: DID NOT - Variable Declaration Parts
mnemonics.content: DID NOT stands for Data type, Identifier (name), Declaration (optional value), NOT initial value (optional) - the components of declaring a variable in C.

mnemonics.title: BAK - Arithmetic Operators Precedence
mnemonics.content: BAK stands for Brackets, Arithmetic (*/%), Addition/Subtraction - the order of precedence for basic arithmetic operators in C expressions.

acronyms.expansion: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
acronyms.usage: Used when understanding char data type storage - each character is stored as a numeric ASCII value (e.g., 'A' = 65, 'a' = 97).
Software like Code::Blocks, Dev-C++ or VS Code used to write, compile and debug C programs containing tokens, variables and data types.
Where variables declared in C programs are stored during execution; understanding RAM helps grasp why data types matter (different sizes).
A constant used in file handling and token recognition; marks the termination point when reading characters/tokens from input.
A unary operator in C used to determine the size in bytes of a data type or variable (e.g., sizeof(int) typically returns 4 bytes).

visualTips.visualization: Picture tokens as individual LEGO bricks. Keywords are red bricks (reserved), identifiers are blue bricks (custom), operators are yellow connectors, constants are green fixed pieces, and punctuation are the connecting plates. Each brick alone does little, but together they build complete programs.

Imagine different-sized containers in a kitchen: char is a small cup (1 byte), int is a medium bowl (4 bytes), float is a large pot (4 bytes), and double is a huge storage bin (8 bytes). Each container holds different amounts/types of data.

Think of variables as cardboard boxes in a warehouse. The data type determines the box size and what it can hold (int box, float box, char box). The variable name is the label on the box. The value inside is what's stored in the box. Each box has a unique location (memory address).

Visualize computer memory as a long street of houses (bytes). Each data type occupies different numbers of houses: char = 1 house, int = 4 houses, double = 8 houses. Variables are assigned specific house numbers (addresses) where their data lives.

analogies.analogies: Tokens are to a C program what words are to a sentence. Just as sentences are made of words, C programs are made of tokens. You cannot write a meaningful sentence without proper words, and you cannot write a working C program without proper tokens arranged correctly.

Data types are like different measurement systems in cooking. A teaspoon (char), a cup (int), a liter (float), and a gallon (double) all measure ingredients but with different capacities. Using the wrong measurement (data type) can ruin your recipe (program) - you can't fit a gallon into a teaspoon.

Variables are like named variables in algebra (x, y, z) but with a physical twist. Imagine x is a mailbox with a label 'x'. The mailbox can hold different letters (values) over time, but the label stays the same. In C, you declare the mailbox type (size) first, then put letters (values) in it.

Choosing a variable identifier is like naming a child - it must follow rules (no numbers first, no existing names/keywords) and should be meaningful. Calling a child '123' or 'if' wouldn't work, just as 'int 123name;' or 'int if;' is invalid in C.

A constant is like the number of days in a week (always 7), while a variable is like your bank balance - it changes. In C, you use 'const' keyword or '#define' to create unchangeable constants, just like laws that cannot be modified once set.
